author: @steve9503
text: I have a quick question. Wouldn't a vertical axis wind turbine be more efficient if it was built so 180 degrees of the vanes/cups were kept out of the wind stream with a baffle of some sort thereby blocking the wind from exerting a force when the vanes/cups face the wind direction?  Like if a water wheel was completely immersed in the river it wouldn't rotate too efficiently.

Another great video Bobert! 
I Believe I have a fun idea for you. Recently I've seen a paintable speaker coil with conductive ink that plays sound when near a magnet, and I thought, "Why not paint a magnet with magnetic ink on a paper below". This would allow for a fully printable speaker on just 2 pieces of paper. If you are interested I can tell you more, if not I'll be testing this in a few weeks myself :) Cheers Mate!

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Good demonstration. The energy in a capacitor E=C*V*V/2. Voltage by itself is pretty much meaningless in terms of energy produced so your critics have a good point. Voltage over a known capacitor does mean something in terms of energy produced so in this experiment you have a good point. As the capacitor nears it's maximum voltage current will drop and energy produced will drop. So will the mechanical load on the hamster wheel.
There is a fun consequence in the formula E=C*V*V/2. If you have 2 same capacitors, one charged to 10V and one uncharged and you short them together charge will devide between them and voltage will be 5V. When you do the math you will find that half of the energy is lost. The thing is that the wire resistance between them does not matter. If the wire resistance is 0.0001 ohm that does happen. If the wire resistance is 1000000000 ohms and it takes a week for the charge to balance that does happen as well. Ain't that funny ?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they do have a good point mate and yes that's funny

text: I saw this sort this sort drum generator before, certain inventor Mike Waters calls it Waters turbine. Basically its the opposite of the dumb propeller design because it takes all the wind in the cross section and harvests it. Some people replaced the props with this and it works in lot slower winds with higher output. Propellers are just the worst design for trubines (Unless the only thing you care about is diameter to weight ratio)

author: @popuptoaster
text: Volts can be useful, I think it's more relevant to put a load across the output, a generator can spin really fast and generate a lot of voltage but as soon as you try to pull amps from it then it will slow down and the voltage drops. You could demonstrate this by hooking your welder up to a petrol generator and listening to it's engine when you strike an arc and the generator has to work harder.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure - but it is really rare to do that - mostly generators are attached to a store and the load pulls from the store - of course the store is the load and that's what is happening here

replies.author: @popuptoaster
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Agreed in many cases although I have my solar set up so the loads pull direct from the panels once the batteries are charged, if I can find a reasonably priced controller with a brake or a decent DIY project I'll do  the same with a turbine once it's added. Generally I try to use any spare power generated to heat water so as not to waste it although the whole system is in bits at the moment. :S

author: @Rich-on6fe
text: That's a Line Feed, not a Carriage Return.

replies.author: @TerryGilsenan
replies.text: Yeah, I smiled at that too. Its a Tape Carriage. A Carriage Return is what happens at the end of a line with a type writer, the typist returns the carriage to the left with the CR/LF lever. :)

author: @PhilWaud
text: Just out of interest, how are commercial generators rated?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Watts at a fixed wind speed - actually you bring up a great point. When assessing  commercial wind generator you just can't believe what is said in the blurb or printed on the box - you need to see the profile which demonstrate watts at given speeds - if you get an estimate of average windspeed in your area it will tell you what a generator will produce over the lifetime
